input_candidates.truth_value: Define composition limits to obtain an optimum microstructural state without embrittling phases, and determine composition ranges for structural applications with UTS ≥ 1100 MPa and impact toughness > 50 J/cm².

input_candidates.truth_value: Typical nominal composition used as baseline.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Systematic study of 31 experimental melts covering Al 3-10%, C 0.3-1.2%, and Mn 24-34%. The optimized composition range for structural applications, accounting for industrial liquation in heavy ingots, is Al 9-9.5%, C 0.9-0.95%, and Mn 28.5-30.5%. Broader fundamental limits without liquation considerations are Al 6.2-9.7%, C 0.7-1%, and Mn 25-31%.

input_candidates.truth_value: Heated to 1050°C, water quenched to room temperature, then aged at 550°C for 16 h.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Induction melting in a 100 kg furnace, electroslag remelting, homogenization at 1150°C for 6-8 h, shaping to circular and square rods, solution treatment at 1050°C with water quenching, and aging at 550°C for 16 h.

input_candidates.truth_value: Used for samples in Fig. 6 (solid curves) and Fig. 7.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Cast ingot-electrodes were repeatedly melted and refined in an electric-slag remelting furnace. Hot working and shaping to rods (12 mm diameter circular; 14 × 14 mm square) were performed after homogenization. For cast steels with up to 12% delta-ferrite, hot working within 1150-1170°C accompanies delta-ferrite dissolution. Jet spraying of liquid metal in argon atmosphere was also mentioned as a method to suppress liquation and avoid delta-ferrite up to 12% Al.

input_candidates.truth_value: Used to determine composition limits for UTS ≥ 1100 MPa and K_CU ≥ 50 J/cm². Resulting optimized ranges: Al 9-9.5%, C 0.9-0.95%, Mn 28.5-30.5%.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Spatial figures limited by surfaces of equal-content levels were plotted. The model established that UTS of at least 1100 MPa together with K_CU of at least 50 J/cm² are attained for C 0.84-0.99%, Al 8.8-9.7%, and Mn 28.5-30.5%. For heavy ingots, narrower practical limits are Al 9-9.5% and C 0.9-0.95%.

input_candidates.truth_value: Fig. 2 shows k-phase (carbide) precipitation along grain boundaries in a steel with 4.6 wt.% Al after aging at 600°C for 16 h, which severely reduces impact toughness.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Fig. 1 data (Al effect, water quench from 1150°C and aging at 550°C for 16 h): For 0.85% C, UTS increases from 800 MPa (3% Al) to 1050 MPa (10% Al); for 0.95% C, UTS increases from 800 MPa (3% Al) to 1250 MPa (10% Al). Ductility remains approximately 45% across the entire Al range for both C levels. U-notch impact toughness (K_CU) peaks at 180 J/cm² for 6-7% Al (0.85% C) and 160 J/cm² for 6-7% Al (0.95% C), dropping sharply to 10 J/cm² at 3% Al (0.85% C) and 7 J/cm² at 3% Al (0.95% C), and to 10 J/cm² at 10% Al (0.85% C) and 60 J/cm² at 10% Al (0.95% C).

input_candidates.truth_value: Fig. 3 reveals d-ferrite morphology in a steel with 10.5 wt.% Al: (a) as-cast state shows d-ferrite; (b) after hot deformation at 1150-1170°C d-ferrite is partially dissolved.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Fig. 5 data (C effect on aging process, Al 9%, Mn 30%): Saturation magnetization (4πJ_s) increases with carbon content and decreases with higher aging temperatures. Representative values after 16 h aging: at 550°C, 0.8% C gives approximately 300 G, 0.92% C gives approximately 600 G, 0.98% C gives approximately 900 G, and 1.06% C gives approximately 1000 G. At 500°C/16h, values range from approximately 150 G (0.8% C) to approximately 750 G (1.06% C). For the same Al content, 4πJ_s increases only up to a certain carbon concentration; raising this limit requires higher Al.

input_candidates.truth_value: Rapid solidification (jet spraying in Ar) produces small spherical particles (<500 µm) and suppresses d-ferrite up to 12% Al (with 0.9% C and 29% Mn). At 14% Al, d-ferrite volume fraction is about 10% (Fig. 4).
input_candidates.prediction_value: Fig. 6 data (C effect on mechanical properties, Al 9%, Mn 30%): The figure presents properties for water-quenched (dashed curves) and aged 550°C/16 h (solid curves) conditions. In the aged state, UTS reaches 1250 MPa at 0.9% C and 1350 MPa at 1.1% C; 0.2% YS reaches 1050 MPa at 0.9% C and 1200 MPa at 1.1% C; ductility is 45% at 0.9% C but drops to 10% at 1.1% C; U-notch impact toughness (K_CU) is 110 J/cm² at 0.9% C and 30 J/cm² at 1.1% C. Carbon concentrations exceeding 1% in the aged state cause grain-boundary carbide precipitation and reduce K_CU by at least 40 J/cm². For C lower than 0.7%, delta-ferrite appears.

input_candidates.truth_value: Fig. 5 reports saturation magnetization 4πJs (in Gauss, G) after aging at various temperatures and times for Fe-9Al-30Mn steels with different C contents. Example values (approximate from graph): 0.8%C aged 450°C/100h ~1500 G, 550°C/16h ~300 G; 1.06%C aged 450°C/100h ~2400 G, 550°C/16h ~1000 G.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Fig. 7 data (Mn effect, Al 9%, C 0.9%, WQ from 1050°C and aged 550°C/16 h): UTS peaks at 1200 MPa for 26% Mn and decreases to 950 MPa for 34% Mn; 0.2% YS is 1000 MPa at 26-28% Mn and 880 MPa at 34% Mn; elongation is highest at 60% for 30% Mn; K_CU is 100-115 J/cm² for Mn 24-32% but drops sharply to 35 J/cm² at 34% Mn due to beta-Mn precipitation after aging.

input_candidates.truth_value: The paper defines composition limits for optimum microstructural state: 6.2% < Al < 9.7%, 0.7% < C < 1.0%, 25% < Mn < 31%.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Microstructural constraints and phase stability: Homogeneous nucleation of kappa-phase during aging at 550°C occurs for Al greater than approximately 7% (the critical Al concentration decreases with higher C). For Al less than approximately 6.2%, carbides precipitate as a continuous network along grain boundaries during aging (Fig. 2), which is highly detrimental to impact toughness. Delta-ferrite appears for Al greater than 10% in cast structures but dissolves during hot working at 1150-1170°C for contents up to approximately 12%. Beta-Mn precipitates after long-time aging at 550°C when Mn exceeds 31-32%, causing extreme brittleness.

input_candidates.truth_value: Further narrowed limits for structural applications: Al 9-9.5%, C 0.9-0.95%, Mn 28.5-30.5%.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Solidification and liquation control: In open-air melting, delta-ferrite increases with ingot weight for Al greater than 9.5%. Electroslag remelting reduces liquation such that delta-ferrite is absent in hot-worked semifinished products for Al less than 9.5% (cross sections up to 105 × 105 mm). Jet spraying of liquid metal in argon atmosphere suppresses liquation and avoids delta-ferrite even at 12% Al (0.9% C, 29% Mn); at 14% Al, delta-ferrite may reach approximately 10% volume fraction after working.
